title: Aggregate & Aggregate Root — Protecting Business Consistency

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
An Aggregate is a cluster of related Entities and Value Objects that are treated as a single unit for maintaining business consistency. Every Aggregate has exactly one Aggregate Root, which controls access to the objects inside the Aggregate.

The Aggregate Root is the only object that external code may reference directly.

sections.heading: Why Aggregates Exist
sections.content:
Business rules often involve multiple objects. If every object could be modified independently, the system could easily become inconsistent.

Order
├── OrderItem
├── Payment
└── Shipment

The Aggregate groups related objects and guarantees that business rules are always preserved.

sections.heading: Aggregate Structure
sections.content:
+---------------------------+
|        Order              |
|     (Aggregate Root)      |
|---------------------------|
| OrderItem                 |
| OrderItem                 |
| Payment                   |
| ShippingAddress           |
+---------------------------+

The Order Aggregate Root owns the entire lifecycle of its internal objects.

sections.heading: Aggregate Root Responsibilities
sections.content:
• Protect business invariants.
  • Validate state transitions.
  • Control modifications.
  • Expose business operations.
  • Publish Domain Events when appropriate.

sections.heading: Business Invariants
sections.content:
An Invariant is a business rule that must always remain true.

Order

✓ Must contain at least one item
✓ Cannot ship before payment
✓ Cannot be paid twice
✓ Cannot cancel after shipping

The Aggregate Root guarantees these rules regardless of who uses the application.

sections.heading: Correct Access
sections.content:
Customer
    │
    ▼
Order.pay()
Order.addItem()
Order.cancel()

All modifications go through the Aggregate Root.

sections.heading: Incorrect Access
sections.content:
order.items[0].price = 0

payment.status = PAID

shipment.status = SHIPPED

Changing internal objects directly bypasses business rules and may leave the Aggregate in an invalid state.

sections.heading: Transaction Boundary
sections.content:
An Aggregate defines the consistency boundary of a transaction.

Database Transaction
        │
        ▼
Entire Aggregate
        │
Commit

Everything inside the Aggregate should remain consistent after the transaction completes.

sections.heading: Aggregate Size
sections.content:
Too SmallToo Large
Business rules spread across multiple Aggregates.High contention and poor scalability.
Difficult consistency.Large transactions.
Frequent coordination.Low performance.

sections.heading: Aggregate vs Database
sections.content:
AggregateDatabase
Business consistency boundary.Persistence technology.
Business concept.Storage implementation.
May span multiple tables.Stores data.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.content:
MistakeConsequence
Very large Aggregates.Poor scalability.
Allowing direct access to child entities.Broken invariants.
Multiple Aggregate Roots.Unclear ownership.
One Aggregate per database table.Database-driven design.

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content: Senior engineers define Aggregates around business consistency, not around object graphs or database relationships. A good Aggregate minimizes transaction size while ensuring that all critical business invariants are always enforced.

sections.heading: Interview Questions
sections.content:
1. What is an Aggregate?
  2. Why does every Aggregate have a Root?
  3. What are business invariants?
  4. Why shouldn't child entities be modified directly?
  5. How do Aggregates define transaction boundaries?
  6. Can an Aggregate span multiple database tables?
  7. How large should an Aggregate be?

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content: An Order Aggregate may contain Order Items, Delivery Address and Payment information. Operations such as adding items, confirming payment or cancelling the order are performed through the Order Aggregate Root, ensuring that pricing, stock reservation and order status remain consistent before business events are published to other contexts.

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• An Aggregate is a consistency boundary.
  • Every Aggregate has exactly one Aggregate Root.
  • Only the Aggregate Root should be accessed externally.
  • Business invariants are protected inside the Aggregate.
  • Aggregate boundaries should be defined by business rules, not by database tables.

title: Bounded Context — Defining Clear Business Boundaries

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
After identifying the business subdomains, the next challenge is deciding where one domain model ends and another begins. Domain-Driven Design solves this problem through the concept of a Bounded Context.

A Bounded Context defines the explicit boundary within which a domain model is valid and its terminology has a precise meaning.

sections.heading: Why Bounded Context Exists
sections.content:
Different parts of the business often use the same word to mean different things.

"Customer"

Sales
▼
Buyer

Support
▼
Person requesting help

Billing
▼
Legal entity responsible for payment

Trying to represent all these meanings with a single Customer class usually creates confusion and unnecessary complexity.

sections.heading: One Business, Multiple Models
sections.content:
Retail Platform

├── Orders
├── Inventory
├── Pricing
├── Shipping
├── Payments

Each business capability has its own rules, terminology and lifecycle. Each deserves its own domain model.

sections.heading: Bounded Context as a Boundary
sections.content:
+----------------------+
| Orders Context       |
|----------------------|
| Order                |
| Customer             |
| OrderItem            |
+----------------------+

+----------------------+
| Shipping Context     |
|----------------------|
| Shipment             |
| Address              |
| Carrier              |
+----------------------+

Each context owns its own model and exposes only what other contexts need.

sections.heading: Models Are Independent
sections.content:
The same business concept may appear differently in different contexts.

OrdersShipping
CustomerRecipient
AddressDelivery Address
Order StatusShipment Status

Each model reflects the needs of its own context.

sections.heading: Bounded Context Is Not a Microservice
sections.content:
Bounded ContextMicroservice
Business boundary.Deployment unit.
Defines a domain model.Defines runtime isolation.
Can exist inside a monolith.Usually deployed independently.

A Modular Monolith can contain several Bounded Contexts.

sections.heading: Bounded Context in a Modular Monolith
sections.content:
Application

├── Orders
├── Inventory
├── Payments
├── Shipping
└── Customers

Each module owns its own entities, repositories and business rules while remaining in the same application.

sections.heading: Bounded Context in Microservices
sections.content:
Orders Service

Inventory Service

Shipping Service

Payments Service

Microservices are one possible implementation strategy for Bounded Contexts, but they are not required.

sections.heading: Communication Between Contexts
sections.content:
Orders
    │
Order Created
    │
    ▼
Inventory
    │
Reserve Stock
    │
    ▼
Shipping

Contexts communicate through explicit contracts instead of sharing internal implementation details.

sections.heading: Signs Your Context Is Too Large
sections.content:
• Many unrelated business concepts.
  • Frequent merge conflicts between teams.
  • Classes with hundreds of methods.
  • Different business experts disagree on terminology.
  • Changes in one feature constantly break another.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.content:
MistakeConsequence
One global domain model.High coupling.
Sharing entities between contexts.Leaky abstractions.
Equating Bounded Context with Microservice.Premature distribution.
Splitting contexts by technical layers.Business boundaries disappear.

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content: Senior engineers identify business boundaries before discussing deployment boundaries. A well-designed Bounded Context can remain inside a Modular Monolith for years and later evolve into a Microservice with minimal changes because the business separation already exists.

sections.heading: Interview Questions
sections.content:
1. What is a Bounded Context?
  2. How does it differ from a Subdomain?
  3. Can multiple Bounded Contexts exist inside one application?
  4. Does every Bounded Context become a Microservice?
  5. Why shouldn't entities be shared across contexts?

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content: A retail platform naturally separates Orders, Pricing, Promotions, Inventory, Logistics and Customer Management into different Bounded Contexts. Each team owns its terminology, business rules and release cadence, allowing the platform to evolve without tightly coupling unrelated business capabilities.

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Bounded Context defines where a domain model is valid.
  • Different contexts may use different models for similar concepts.
  • Bounded Context is a business boundary, not a deployment strategy.
  • Multiple Bounded Contexts can coexist inside a Modular Monolith.
  • Strong business boundaries reduce coupling and improve maintainability.

title: Putting Everything Together — Designing an Order Management System with DDD

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
After learning the individual building blocks of Domain-Driven Design, the next step is understanding how they fit together in a real application. This chapter combines every concept presented so far into a single Order Management System.

DDD is not a collection of patterns. It is a way of modelling business complexity.

sections.heading: Business Problem
sections.content: An online retailer allows customers to purchase products. The system must calculate prices, reserve inventory, process payments and arrange shipment while keeping business rules consistent.

sections.heading: Step 1 — Ubiquitous Language
sections.content:
Customer
Order
Order Item
Product
Payment
Shipment
Warehouse
Invoice

These terms are shared by developers, testers and business experts.

sections.heading: Step 2 — Subdomains
sections.content:
Retail Platform

├── Orders
├── Inventory
├── Payments
├── Shipping
├── Pricing
└── Customer Management

Each subdomain represents a different business capability.

sections.heading: Step 4 — Context Map
sections.content:
Orders
   │
OrderPlaced
   ▼
Inventory
   │
StockReserved
   ▼
Payments
   │
PaymentApproved
   ▼
Shipping

Contexts communicate through explicit business events instead of sharing implementation details.

sections.heading: Step 5 — Order Aggregate
sections.content:
Order (Aggregate Root)

├── OrderItem
├── ShippingAddress
└── PaymentStatus

The Aggregate Root guarantees business consistency.

sections.heading: Step 6 — Value Objects
sections.content:
Money
Address
EmailAddress
Quantity

These objects have no identity and are compared by value.

sections.heading: Step 7 — Repository
sections.content:
Application Service
        │
        ▼
OrderRepository
        │
        ▼
PostgreSQL

The Repository persists the complete Aggregate without exposing persistence details.

sections.heading: Step 8 — Application Service
sections.content:
PlaceOrder

Load Customer
Load Product
Create Order
Save Order
Publish Event

The Application Service orchestrates the use case without implementing business rules.

sections.heading: Step 9 — Domain Service
sections.content:
PricingService

Calculate Discounts
Apply Promotions
Calculate Taxes

Business logic spanning multiple concepts belongs in a Domain Service.

sections.heading: Step 10 — Domain Events
sections.content:
OrderPlaced
      │
      ├── Reserve Stock
      ├── Send Email
      ├── Generate Invoice
      └── Update Analytics

Domain Events allow other Bounded Contexts to react without creating tight coupling.

sections.heading: Complete Architecture
sections.content:
Customer
                    │
                    ▼
            Application Service
                    │
                    ▼
           Order Aggregate Root
        ┌─────────┼──────────┐
        ▼         ▼          ▼
   OrderItem   Address    Payment
                    │
                    ▼
             OrderRepository
                    │
                    ▼
               PostgreSQL
                    │
                    ▼
             OrderPlaced Event
          ┌────────┼─────────┬──────────┐
          ▼        ▼         ▼          ▼
     Inventory  Shipping  Invoice  Notification

sections.heading: How This Evolves
sections.content:
ArchitectureChanges Required
Layered ArchitectureNone.
Modular MonolithEach Bounded Context becomes a module.
Hexagonal ArchitectureRepositories become Ports.
MicroservicesEach Bounded Context can become an independent service.
Event-Driven ArchitectureDomain Events become Integration Events.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.content:
MistakeWhy It Is Wrong
One Repository per table.Repositories persist Aggregates.
Business rules in Controllers.Controllers orchestrate HTTP.
Business rules in Application Services.Creates an Anemic Domain Model.
Sharing Entities between contexts.Violates Bounded Context boundaries.
Publishing events before commit.Can produce inconsistent systems.

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content: Senior engineers model business capabilities before discussing frameworks or infrastructure. The same DDD model can be implemented as a layered application, a Modular Monolith or a Microservices architecture. The domain model remains stable while the technical architecture evolves to meet scalability, deployment and operational requirements.

sections.heading: Interview Questions
sections.content:
1. Walk through the lifecycle of placing an order using DDD.
  2. Where would you place pricing logic?
  3. Why is Order the Aggregate Root?
  4. Where are business invariants enforced?
  5. How do Domain Events reduce coupling?
  6. How would this design evolve into Microservices?
  7. How would Hexagonal Architecture fit into this design?

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content: A retail platform such as REWE naturally separates Orders, Inventory, Pricing, Logistics and Customer Management into different Bounded Contexts. Each team owns its Aggregate Roots, Repositories and business rules while communicating through business events. This approach supports independent evolution of business capabilities and provides a solid foundation for modular monoliths or microservices.

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• DDD starts with the business domain, not the database.
  • Entities, Value Objects and Aggregates model business concepts.
  • Repositories persist Aggregates.
  • Application Services orchestrate use cases.
  • Domain Services implement cross-Aggregate business rules.
  • Domain Events enable loose coupling.
  • The same domain model can support multiple architectural styles.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
Bounded Contexts intentionally separate business models, but business capabilities still need to collaborate. A customer places an order, inventory reserves products, payment authorises the transaction and shipping delivers the package. Domain-Driven Design introduces the Context Map to describe how these independent contexts communicate.

A Context Map documents the relationships between Bounded Contexts rather than their internal implementation.

sections.heading: Why Context Maps Exist
sections.content:
Without explicit integration rules, teams gradually share entities, databases and internal APIs until all contexts become tightly coupled.

Orders
   │
   ├── Shared Database
   ├── Shared Entities
   └── Direct SQL

Result:
High Coupling

Context Maps define clean integration boundaries.

sections.heading: A Typical Retail Example
sections.content:
Customer
    │
    ▼
Orders
    │
Order Created
    ▼
Inventory
    │
Stock Reserved
    ▼
Shipping
    │
Shipment Created
    ▼
Notification

Each context owns its own model while collaborating through well-defined contracts.

sections.heading: Context Relationships
sections.content:
DDD defines several integration patterns describing how contexts depend on one another.

RelationshipPurpose
PartnershipTeams collaborate closely.
Customer / SupplierOne context provides capabilities consumed by another.
ConformistThe downstream context accepts the upstream model.
Shared KernelContexts share a carefully controlled subset of the model.
Anti-Corruption LayerProtects one model from another.
Open Host ServiceProvides stable integration interfaces.
Published LanguageShared contracts used for communication.

sections.heading: Customer / Supplier
sections.content:
Pricing
    │
Provides Prices
    ▼
Orders

The supplier owns the contract while the customer depends on it.

sections.heading: Shared Kernel
sections.content:
Orders
      ▲
      │
Shared Types
      │
      ▼
Shipping

Only a very small and carefully maintained part of the model should be shared.

sections.heading: Anti-Corruption Layer
sections.content:
Legacy ERP
      │
Translator
      │
Orders

An Anti-Corruption Layer prevents external models from leaking into your own domain model.

sections.heading: Published Language
sections.content:
OrderCreated
OrderCancelled
PaymentApproved
ShipmentCreated

Contexts exchange contracts instead of exposing internal implementation details.

sections.heading: Open Host Service
sections.content:
REST API
GraphQL
Kafka Topics
Public Events

A context exposes stable integration points that other contexts can safely consume.

sections.heading: Choosing the Right Relationship
sections.content:
SituationRecommended Relationship
Legacy IntegrationAnti-Corruption Layer
Independent TeamsPublished Language
Strong CollaborationPartnership
Small Shared ModelShared Kernel

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.content:
MistakeConsequence
Sharing the same database.Strong coupling.
Sharing JPA entities.Leaky domain models.
Calling internal services directly.Broken boundaries.
No documented integration strategy.Architecture erosion.

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content: Experienced architects spend as much time designing the relationships between Bounded Contexts as they do designing the contexts themselves. Most scalability and maintainability problems originate from poorly defined integrations rather than poor object modelling.

sections.heading: Interview Questions
sections.content:
1. What is a Context Map?
  2. How does it differ from a Bounded Context?
  3. When would you use an Anti-Corruption Layer?
  4. What is a Shared Kernel?
  5. Should contexts share entities?
  6. What is a Published Language?

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content: A retail platform integrates Orders, Inventory, Pricing, Logistics and Customer Management. Each context owns its business model while exchanging business events such as OrderCreated, StockReserved and ShipmentCreated through stable contracts. This allows teams to evolve independently without exposing internal implementation details.

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Context Maps describe relationships between Bounded Contexts.
  • Contexts should collaborate through explicit contracts.
  • Avoid sharing entities or databases across contexts.
  • Integration patterns reduce coupling while preserving business autonomy.
  • Well-designed Context Maps enable modular monoliths and microservices alike.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
A Domain Event represents something important that has already happened in the business domain. It is not a command or a request—it is a record of a completed business fact.

Commands express intent. Domain Events express facts.

sections.heading: Why Domain Events Exist
sections.content:
Business actions often trigger additional processes. When an order is placed, inventory must reserve stock, notifications must be sent and loyalty points may be awarded. Without Domain Events, the Order Aggregate would become tightly coupled to every other business capability.

Place Order
      │
      ▼
Order Paid
      │
      ├── Reserve Stock
      ├── Send Email
      ├── Update Analytics
      └── Generate Invoice

sections.heading: Command vs Domain Event
sections.content:
CommandDomain Event
PlaceOrderOrderPlaced
PayOrderOrderPaid
CancelOrderOrderCancelled
ShipOrderOrderShipped

A command asks the system to do something. A Domain Event states that something already happened.

sections.heading: Event Flow
sections.content:
User
   │
Command
   │
   ▼
Application Service
   │
   ▼
Order Aggregate
   │
Business Rule
   │
   ▼
OrderPaid Event
   │
   ├── Inventory
   ├── Shipping
   ├── Notification
   └── Analytics

sections.heading: Characteristics of Domain Events
sections.content:
• Represent completed business facts.
  • Are immutable.
  • Use past-tense names.
  • Contain only relevant business information.
  • Can have multiple consumers.

sections.heading: Naming Conventions
sections.content:
GoodPoor
OrderPlacedCreateOrder
PaymentApprovedApprovePayment
ShipmentCreatedCreateShipment
CustomerRegisteredRegisterCustomer

Events describe facts that already occurred.

sections.heading: Publishing Domain Events
sections.content:
Order Aggregate
      │
order.pay()
      │
Business succeeds
      │
      ▼
OrderPaid Event

Domain Events are typically created after business rules have been successfully applied.

sections.heading: Domain Events vs Integration Events
sections.content:
Domain EventIntegration Event
Internal to the domain.Shared with external systems.
Rich business model.Stable public contract.
May evolve frequently.Should evolve carefully.

Many systems translate Domain Events into Integration Events before publishing them outside the bounded context.

sections.heading: Benefits
sections.content:
• Loose coupling.
  • Better scalability.
  • Independent evolution of contexts.
  • Simpler business workflows.
  • Natural fit for Event-Driven Architecture.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.content:
MistakeConsequence
Naming events as commands.Confusing intent with facts.
Publishing before the transaction succeeds.Inconsistent systems.
Putting infrastructure data inside events.Leaky abstractions.
Using events for synchronous workflows.Unnecessary complexity.

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content: Domain Events should model business facts, not technical implementation details. In distributed systems, they are frequently translated into Integration Events and delivered through messaging platforms such as Kafka, RabbitMQ or cloud event buses. Reliable publication often requires patterns such as the Transactional Outbox to avoid losing events when a database transaction succeeds but message publication fails.

sections.heading: Interview Questions
sections.content:
1. What is a Domain Event?
  2. How is a Domain Event different from a Command?
  3. Why should events use past-tense names?
  4. When should a Domain Event be published?
  5. What is the difference between a Domain Event and an Integration Event?
  6. How do Domain Events reduce coupling?
  7. Why is the Outbox Pattern commonly used with Domain Events?

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content: When an Order is successfully paid, the Order Aggregate may produce an OrderPaid Domain Event. Inside the Orders bounded context this event represents a completed business fact. It can later be translated into an Integration Event consumed by Inventory, Logistics and Notification services, allowing each business capability to evolve independently while maintaining clear ownership of business rules.

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Domain Events represent completed business facts.
  • Commands express intent; events express outcomes.
  • Events are immutable and use past-tense names.
  • Domain Events reduce coupling between Aggregates and Bounded Contexts.
  • In distributed systems, Domain Events often become Integration Events delivered through messaging infrastructure.

title: Entity — Objects with Identity and Lifecycle

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
In Domain-Driven Design, an Entity is an object defined primarily by its identity rather than by its attributes. While its state may change throughout its lifecycle, its identity remains constant.

An Entity answers the question: Which real-world thing does this object represent?

sections.heading: Why Entities Exist
sections.content:
Many business concepts evolve over time. A customer changes address, an order changes status, an employee gets promoted. Although their data changes, they are still the same business object.

Customer #1023

Address: London
▼
Address: Madrid

Same Customer

sections.heading: Identity vs Attributes
sections.content:
IdentityAttributes
Never changes.May change.
Uniquely identifies the Entity.Describe the current state.
Usually an ID.Name, Address, Status...

sections.heading: Examples
sections.content:
EntityIdentity
CustomercustomerId
OrderorderId
InvoiceinvoiceId
ShipmentshipmentId
EmployeeemployeeId

sections.heading: Lifecycle
sections.content:
Create
   │
   ▼
Update
   │
   ▼
Suspend
   │
   ▼
Reactivate
   │
   ▼
Archive

An Entity normally has a lifecycle. It is created, modified and eventually removed or archived.

sections.heading: Rich Domain Model
sections.content:
Entities should contain behaviour, not only data.

Order

pay()
ship()
cancel()
calculateTotal()

The Entity is responsible for protecting its own business rules.

sections.heading: Anemic Domain Model
sections.content:
An Anemic Domain Model contains only fields and getters/setters while business rules are scattered across services.

Order

id
status
total

getters
setters

This makes the domain model weak and difficult to maintain.

sections.heading: Rich vs Anemic
sections.content:
Rich Domain ModelAnemic Domain Model
Contains behaviour.Contains only data.
Protects business rules.Rules spread across services.
High cohesion.Low cohesion.
Preferred in DDD.Generally considered an anti-pattern in DDD.

sections.heading: Entity or Value Object?
sections.content:
QuestionIf YES
Does it have a business identity?Entity
Does it have its own lifecycle?Entity
Is it tracked independently?Entity
Can it simply be replaced?Probably a Value Object.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.content:
MistakeConsequence
Using Entities everywhere.Complex model.
Entities with only getters/setters.Anemic Domain Model.
Putting business rules in services.Low cohesion.
Comparing Entities by attributes.Incorrect identity semantics.

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content: Experienced developers keep business behaviour close to the data it affects. An Entity should be responsible for maintaining its own consistency instead of relying on external services to manipulate its internal state.

sections.heading: Interview Questions
sections.content:
1. What is an Entity in DDD?
  2. How is an Entity different from a Value Object?
  3. Why is identity more important than attributes?
  4. What is an Anemic Domain Model?
  5. Should Entities contain business behaviour?
  6. How should two Entities be compared?

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content: In a retail platform, entities such as Customer, Order, Shipment and Product are identified by unique business identifiers and evolve over time. Their behaviour includes operations such as placing orders, reserving stock, changing delivery addresses and cancelling shipments while preserving business consistency.

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• An Entity is defined by its identity.
  • Its attributes may change during its lifecycle.
  • Entities should contain business behaviour.
  • Avoid the Anemic Domain Model.
  • Identity distinguishes an Entity from a Value Object.

title: Why Domain-Driven Design Exists — Solving Complex Business Problems

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
Most software projects do not fail because developers cannot write code. They fail because the code no longer represents the business it is supposed to support. As requirements evolve, business rules become scattered across controllers, services, repositories and database procedures until the application becomes difficult to understand and even harder to change.

Domain-Driven Design (DDD) exists to keep software aligned with business knowledge as systems grow in size and complexity.

sections.heading: The Real Problem
sections.content:
Imagine an e-commerce platform. At first, creating an order appears simple: receive products, calculate the total and save the order.

Create Order
      │
      ▼
Calculate Total
      │
      ▼
Save

Months later the business introduces promotions, coupons, taxes, regional pricing, multiple payment methods, inventory reservations, fraud detection and shipping rules. What was once a simple service gradually becomes a large collection of conditional statements.

sections.heading: The Growth of Complexity
sections.content:
OrderService

if VIP...
if Coupon...
if Black Friday...
if International...
if Subscription...
if Premium Delivery...
if Marketplace...
if Digital Product...
...

Complexity rarely appears suddenly. It grows feature after feature until understanding the application becomes more difficult than implementing new functionality.

sections.heading: The Anemic Domain Model
sections.content:
A common anti-pattern is the Anemic Domain Model, where domain objects contain only data while all business rules are implemented elsewhere.

Customer

id
name
email
points

The Customer object knows nothing about customers. Every business rule lives inside services, making behaviour difficult to discover and maintain.

sections.heading: Why Layered Architectures Become Difficult
sections.content:
Controller
      │
      ▼
Service
      │
      ▼
Repository
      │
      ▼
Database

Layered architectures organize technical responsibilities well, but they do not automatically organize business knowledge. As applications grow, business logic tends to leak across multiple layers.

sections.heading: DDD Changes the Focus
sections.content:
Instead of asking how to organize code, DDD starts by asking how the business works.

Business Experts
        │
        ▼
Business Model
        │
        ▼
Software Model
        │
        ▼
Implementation

The software becomes a representation of the business rather than a collection of database operations.

sections.heading: The Core Idea
sections.content:
The primary goal of Domain-Driven Design is not better code. It is better understanding of the business domain.

When developers and domain experts share the same mental model, software becomes easier to evolve because the implementation reflects business concepts instead of technical shortcuts.

sections.heading: What DDD Is Not
sections.content:
MythReality
DDD is a framework.DDD is a design philosophy.
DDD requires microservices.DDD works equally well with modular monoliths and microservices.
DDD replaces Clean Architecture.DDD models the business domain, while architectural patterns organize application structure.
DDD is only for large companies.Any application with complex business rules can benefit from DDD.

sections.heading: DDD Building Blocks
sections.content:
Domain Knowledge
        │
        ▼
Ubiquitous Language
        │
        ▼
Entities
Value Objects
Aggregates
Repositories
Domain Services
Domain Events
Bounded Contexts

These concepts are introduced to solve specific business modelling problems rather than to increase architectural complexity.

sections.heading: A Running Example
sections.content:
Throughout this module we will use the same e-commerce platform to illustrate every DDD concept.

Customer
      │
      ▼
Order
      │
      ▼
Payment
      │
      ▼
Inventory
      │
      ▼
Shipment

Each chapter will progressively evolve this domain model using Domain-Driven Design principles.

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content: Senior engineers recognise that most long-term software problems are modelling problems rather than programming problems. Choosing the correct domain boundaries usually has a greater impact on maintainability than selecting a particular framework or database technology.

sections.heading: Interview Questions
sections.content:
1. Why was Domain-Driven Design created?
  2. What kinds of systems benefit most from DDD?
  3. What problem does the Anemic Domain Model create?
  4. Does DDD require microservices?
  5. How does DDD differ from Clean Architecture?
  6. Can DDD be applied to a modular monolith?

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content: Large retail platforms manage orders, products, pricing, promotions, logistics, suppliers and customer accounts. Each area contains distinct business rules that evolve independently. Domain-Driven Design helps engineering teams model these domains explicitly, reducing coupling between teams and allowing each business capability to evolve without impacting the entire platform.

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• DDD addresses business complexity, not technical complexity.
  • The business domain should drive the software model.
  • Business knowledge belongs inside the domain model.
  • DDD complements architectural patterns such as Clean Architecture and Hexagonal Architecture.
  • Understanding the domain comes before choosing technologies.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
A Repository provides the illusion of an in-memory collection of Aggregates. It hides persistence details and allows the domain model to work without knowing whether data comes from PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis or another storage technology.

A Repository belongs to the Domain Layer, while its implementation belongs to the Infrastructure Layer.

sections.heading: Why Repositories Exist
sections.content:
Business code should not depend on SQL, JPA, Hibernate or any persistence framework.

Application
      │
      ▼
Repository
      │
      ▼
Database

The domain only knows the Repository interface.

sections.heading: Repository Responsibilities
sections.content:
• Load Aggregates.
  • Persist Aggregates.
  • Remove Aggregates.
  • Hide persistence technology.
  • Provide business-oriented queries.

sections.heading: Repository Works with Aggregates
sections.content:
✔ OrderRepository

save(Order)
findById(orderId)
delete(Order)

❌ OrderItemRepository
❌ PaymentRepository

Repositories are created for Aggregate Roots, not for every Entity inside an Aggregate.

sections.heading: Repository vs DAO
sections.content:
RepositoryDAO
Domain concept.Infrastructure concept.
Works with Aggregates.Works with database records.
Business-oriented.Persistence-oriented.
Hides storage details.Exposes storage operations.

sections.heading: Repository Interface
sections.content:
OrderRepository

findById(id)
save(order)
delete(order)

The interface expresses business intent, not database operations.

sections.heading: Implementation
sections.content:
Domain
│
├── OrderRepository (interface)
│
Infrastructure
│
└── JpaOrderRepository
└── JdbcOrderRepository
└── MongoOrderRepository

Multiple implementations may exist without affecting the domain model.

sections.heading: One Repository per Aggregate Root
sections.content:
Aggregate RootRepository
OrderOrderRepository
CustomerCustomerRepository
InvoiceInvoiceRepository

Child entities are accessed through their Aggregate Root.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.content:
MistakeConsequence
Repository for every table.Database-driven design.
Returning JPA entities everywhere.Infrastructure leaks into the domain.
Repositories containing business logic.Violates separation of concerns.
Repositories with dozens of search methods.Bloated interfaces.

sections.heading: Repository in Hexagonal Architecture
sections.content:
Application Service
        │
        ▼
OrderRepository (Port)
        │
        ▼
JpaOrderRepository (Adapter)
        │
        ▼
PostgreSQL

In Hexagonal Architecture, the Repository interface is an outbound port, while the persistence implementation is an adapter.

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content: Experienced engineers keep Repositories focused on Aggregate persistence. Complex reporting, analytics and read-heavy queries are often implemented outside the domain model using dedicated read models or CQRS, preventing the Repository from becoming a generic data-access layer.

sections.heading: Interview Questions
sections.content:
1. What is a Repository?
  2. How is a Repository different from a DAO?
  3. Why is there one Repository per Aggregate Root?
  4. Should a Repository contain business logic?
  5. Where is the Repository interface located?
  6. Where is its implementation located?

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content: An OrderRepository loads and persists complete Order Aggregates rather than individual database rows. The business layer remains independent from Hibernate or PostgreSQL, allowing persistence technology to evolve without changing the domain model.

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• A Repository persists Aggregates, not individual Entities.
  • The interface belongs to the Domain Layer.
  • The implementation belongs to the Infrastructure Layer.
  • Repositories are business-oriented, not database-oriented.
  • Avoid treating Repositories as generic DAOs.

title: Domain Service vs Application Service — Where Business Logic Belongs

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
Not every business operation naturally belongs inside an Entity or Value Object. Domain-Driven Design introduces Domain Services for domain logic that spans multiple Aggregates or cannot reasonably belong to a single object. At the same time, Application Services coordinate use cases, repositories and external systems without containing business rules.

Domain Services implement business decisions. Application Services orchestrate business operations.

sections.heading: Why Services Exist
sections.content:
Some business operations involve multiple Aggregates or external systems. Placing this logic inside an Entity would create unnecessary coupling.

Customer
     │
     ├── Order
     ├── Payment
     └── Inventory

Business operation
      │
      ▼
Domain Service

sections.heading: Application Service Responsibilities
sections.content:
• Execute a use case.
  • Load Aggregates from Repositories.
  • Call Domain methods.
  • Persist Aggregates.
  • Publish Domain Events.
  • Coordinate external services.

sections.heading: Domain Service Responsibilities
sections.content:
• Implement domain rules.
  • Coordinate multiple Aggregates.
  • Contain business decisions.
  • Remain independent of infrastructure.

sections.heading: Execution Flow
sections.content:
REST Controller
        │
        ▼
Application Service
        │
        ▼
Repositories
        │
        ▼
Aggregates
        │
        ▼
Domain Service (if needed)
        │
        ▼
Save Aggregate
        │
        ▼
Publish Domain Event

sections.heading: When an Entity Is Enough
sections.content:
order.pay()
order.cancel()
order.ship()

If the behaviour naturally belongs to a single Aggregate, keep it inside the Aggregate Root.

sections.heading: When a Domain Service Is Appropriate
sections.content:
Transfer Inventory

Warehouse A
        │
        ▼
Warehouse B

The operation involves multiple Aggregates and business rules that do not naturally belong to only one of them.

sections.heading: Domain Service vs Application Service
sections.content:
Domain ServiceApplication Service
Contains business rules.Coordinates the workflow.
Part of the Domain Layer.Part of the Application Layer.
Independent from infrastructure.Uses repositories and external services.
Expresses business concepts.Implements use cases.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.content:
MistakeConsequence
Putting all business logic in Application Services.Anemic Domain Model.
Creating Domain Services for everything.God Services.
Repositories inside Entities.Infrastructure leaks into the Domain.
Business rules in Controllers.Poor separation of concerns.

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content: A useful rule is: if a business rule can naturally live inside an Aggregate, keep it there. Create a Domain Service only when the rule genuinely spans multiple Aggregates or cannot logically belong to a single one. Application Services should remain thin, acting primarily as orchestrators.

sections.heading: Interview Questions
sections.content:
1. What is the difference between a Domain Service and an Application Service?
  2. Should Application Services contain business rules?
  3. When should you introduce a Domain Service?
  4. Why should Entities not access Repositories directly?
  5. What layer owns the Domain Service?
  6. What layer owns the Application Service?

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content: An Application Service may coordinate the 'Place Order' use case by loading the Customer and Order Aggregates, invoking domain operations, saving changes and publishing an OrderCreated event. A Domain Service could calculate complex pricing or promotional discounts involving multiple business concepts without depending on infrastructure.

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Application Services orchestrate use cases.
  • Domain Services implement domain rules that do not belong to a single Aggregate.
  • Keep business behaviour inside Aggregates whenever possible.
  • Avoid turning Application Services into large procedural classes.
  • Thin Application Services and rich Domain Models are a hallmark of well-designed DDD systems.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
Large businesses solve many different problems at the same time. An online retailer does not only sell products. It manages inventory, pricing, logistics, suppliers, payments, promotions, customer accounts and many other capabilities. Domain-Driven Design recommends dividing this complexity into Subdomains, each representing a distinct business capability.

Large software systems become manageable when business complexity is divided into smaller domains.

sections.heading: Why Subdomains Exist
sections.content:
Business
    │
    ▼
Many Business Capabilities
    │
    ▼
Independent Subdomains
    │
    ▼
Independent Software Models

Instead of modelling the entire company as one massive system, DDD encourages modelling each business capability independently.

sections.heading: Example — Retail Platform
sections.content:
Retail Platform

├── Product Catalog
├── Pricing
├── Inventory
├── Orders
├── Payments
├── Shipping
├── Customer Accounts
├── Promotions
└── Suppliers

Each of these areas solves different business problems and evolves independently.

sections.heading: Business Capabilities Have Different Rules
sections.content:
SubdomainMain Responsibility
OrdersCreate and manage customer orders.
InventoryTrack product availability.
PricingCalculate product prices.
ShippingDeliver purchased products.
PaymentsProcess financial transactions.

Each capability has its own business rules and should evolve without tightly coupling to the others.

sections.heading: Types of Subdomains
sections.content:
TypeDescription
Core DomainThe business capability that provides competitive advantage.
Supporting SubdomainBusiness functionality required to support the Core Domain.
Generic SubdomainCommon capabilities available in many systems.

sections.heading: Core Domain
sections.content:
The Core Domain contains the business logic that differentiates one company from another.

Retail Company
        │
        ▼
Pricing Strategy
Recommendation Engine
Promotion Rules

This is usually where the company invests most of its engineering effort.

sections.heading: Supporting Subdomains
sections.content:
Supporting Subdomains implement business-specific functionality but are not the primary competitive advantage.

Returns
Customer Support
Supplier Management

sections.heading: Generic Subdomains
sections.content:
Generic Subdomains solve common problems that almost every business has.

Authentication
Notifications
Email
Logging
Audit

These are often implemented using existing frameworks or third-party services.

sections.heading: Why This Separation Matters
sections.content:
Business
      │
      ▼
Subdomains
      │
      ▼
Bounded Contexts
      │
      ▼
Software Modules

Subdomains provide the business perspective that later becomes Bounded Contexts inside the software architecture.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.content:
MistakeConsequence
One large domain model.High coupling.
Split by technical layers.Business concepts become mixed.
Everything is a Core Domain.Engineering effort is wasted.
Ignoring business boundaries.Difficult maintenance.

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content: Senior engineers begin modelling by identifying business capabilities rather than database tables or REST endpoints. Correct subdomain identification reduces coupling, simplifies team ownership and creates a foundation for scalable architectures such as Modular Monoliths and Microservices.

sections.heading: Interview Questions
sections.content:
1. What is a Subdomain?
  2. Why are Subdomains important in DDD?
  3. What is the difference between Core, Supporting and Generic Subdomains?
  4. Should every Subdomain become a Microservice?
  5. How do Subdomains relate to Bounded Contexts?

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content: A retail platform naturally separates into Product Catalog, Pricing, Promotions, Inventory, Orders, Logistics, Payments and Customer Accounts. Each capability evolves independently, has different business experts and follows different business rules. Identifying these Subdomains allows engineering teams to organise software around business capabilities instead of technical layers.

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Subdomains divide business complexity.
  • Every Subdomain represents a distinct business capability.
  • Core Domains contain competitive advantage.
  • Supporting and Generic Subdomains reduce unnecessary complexity.
  • Subdomains provide the foundation for Bounded Contexts.

title: Ubiquitous Language — Speaking the Language of the Business

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
One of the main causes of software complexity is the mismatch between the language used by business experts and the language used by developers. Domain-Driven Design introduces the concept of a Ubiquitous Language: a common vocabulary shared by developers, domain experts, testers, analysts and stakeholders.

If two people describe the same business concept using different words, misunderstandings become inevitable.

sections.heading: The Communication Problem
sections.content:
Business Expert
"Reserve a product"

Developer
"Create inventory record"

QA
"Lock stock"

Database
reserved_quantity

Although everyone refers to the same business concept, different terminology leads to confusion and inconsistent implementations.

sections.heading: The Goal of Ubiquitous Language
sections.content:
Business Experts
        │
        ▼
Shared Vocabulary
        │
        ▼
Software Model
        │
        ▼
Source Code

The same words used during business meetings should appear in diagrams, documentation, source code and automated tests.

sections.heading: Code Should Reflect the Business
sections.content:
Names in the source code should represent business concepts rather than technical shortcuts.

PoorBetter
ProcessData()ApproveOrder()
EntityManagerOrderRepository
RecordShipment
FlagOrderStatus

sections.heading: A Shared Mental Model
sections.content:
Customer
      │
places
      ▼
Order
      │
contains
      ▼
Order Items
      │
paid by
      ▼
Payment

Every participant should understand these concepts in exactly the same way.

sections.heading: Business Terms vs Technical Terms
sections.content:
Technical ThinkingBusiness Thinking
Insert RowCreate Order
Update StatusApprove Payment
Delete RecordCancel Reservation
Foreign KeyCustomer owns Orders

DDD encourages modelling business behaviour rather than database operations.

sections.heading: Avoid Synonyms
sections.content:
Every important business concept should have a single agreed name.

Customer
Client
Buyer
Account Holder

Choosing one term avoids ambiguity across the codebase.

sections.heading: Language Evolves
sections.content: The ubiquitous language is not fixed. As the business evolves, new concepts emerge and existing terminology changes. The software model should evolve together with the business vocabulary.

sections.heading: Example — Online Retail
sections.content:
Customer
Product
Shopping Cart
Order
Payment
Shipment
Invoice
Refund
Promotion
Coupon

These are business concepts, not database tables. Their names should remain consistent throughout the application.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.content:
MistakeConsequence
Different names for the same concept.Miscommunication.
Technical names in the domain model.Business becomes difficult to understand.
Database names drive the design.Business model becomes hidden.
Abbreviations everywhere.Lower readability.

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content: Senior engineers spend significant time discussing terminology with domain experts before writing code. Correct names reduce misunderstandings, simplify onboarding and make the software easier to evolve over many years.

sections.heading: Interview Questions
sections.content:
1. What is Ubiquitous Language?
  2. Why is a shared vocabulary important?
  3. Should source code use technical or business terminology?
  4. Can the Ubiquitous Language change over time?
  5. How does Ubiquitous Language improve maintainability?

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content: In a retail platform, terms such as Order, Promotion, Basket, Delivery Slot, Warehouse, Supplier and Shipment have precise business meanings. Using the same terminology across teams, services, APIs and databases reduces ambiguity and enables different engineering teams to collaborate without constantly translating between technical and business language.

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Ubiquitous Language is a shared vocabulary between business and engineering.
  • The same terminology should appear in conversations, documentation and source code.
  • Business concepts should drive class names, methods and APIs.
  • Avoid synonyms for the same concept.
  • The language evolves together with the business.

title: Value Object — Objects Defined by Their Values

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
A Value Object is an object that is identified only by the values it contains. Unlike an Entity, it has no business identity and no independent lifecycle. If its values change, a new Value Object is created.

A Value Object answers the question: What describes something?, not Which thing is it?.

sections.heading: Why Value Objects Exist
sections.content:
Many business concepts describe characteristics rather than independent business objects. An address, a money amount or an email address does not usually need its own identity.

Customer
   │
   ├── Name
   ├── Email
   └── Address

Address describes the Customer.
It is not the Customer.

sections.heading: Identity vs Value
sections.content:
EntityValue Object
Has identity.No identity.
Has lifecycle.No lifecycle.
Mutable.Usually immutable.
Compared by ID.Compared by value.

sections.heading: Common Examples
sections.content:
Value ObjectRepresents
AddressLocation information.
MoneyAmount + Currency.
EmailAddressValidated email.
PhoneNumberValidated phone number.
CoordinatesLatitude and Longitude.

sections.heading: Immutability
sections.content:
Value Objects are typically immutable. Instead of modifying an existing object, a new one is created.

Address

Gran Via 10
Madrid
Spain

▼

New Address

Gran Via 15
Madrid
Spain

This prevents unexpected side effects and makes the model easier to reason about.

sections.heading: Equality
sections.content:
Two Value Objects are equal if all their values are equal.

Money(100, EUR)
==
Money(100, EUR)

true

Unlike Entities, identity is irrelevant.

sections.heading: Encapsulating Business Rules
sections.content:
Value Objects should validate and protect their own invariants.

EmailAddress

validateFormat()
normalize()
domain()

This prevents invalid values from entering the domain model.

sections.heading: Entity vs Value Object
sections.content:
QuestionAnswer
Needs its own identity?Entity.
Has independent lifecycle?Entity.
Only describes something?Value Object.
Can be replaced entirely?Value Object.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.content:
MistakeConsequence
Creating IDs for Value Objects.Unnecessary complexity.
Making Value Objects mutable.Hidden side effects.
Using primitives everywhere.Business rules become scattered.
Comparing by reference.Incorrect equality semantics.

sections.heading: Senior Perspective
sections.content: Experienced developers replace primitive types with meaningful Value Objects whenever those values have business rules. Instead of passing strings or decimals everywhere, they create concepts such as EmailAddress, Money or Percentage, making the code safer and more expressive.

sections.heading: Interview Questions
sections.content:
1. What is a Value Object?
  2. How is it different from an Entity?
  3. Why are Value Objects usually immutable?
  4. How should Value Objects be compared?
  5. Can a Value Object contain business logic?
  6. When would you replace a primitive with a Value Object?

sections.heading: REWE Perspective
sections.content: Retail systems commonly model concepts such as Money, Address, EmailAddress, Quantity and Weight as Value Objects. These concepts encapsulate validation and business rules while avoiding unnecessary identities and database tables.

sections.heading: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
• Value Objects are identified by their values.
  • They usually have no identity or lifecycle.
  • Immutability simplifies reasoning and improves safety.
  • Compare Value Objects by value, not by reference.
  • Encapsulate business rules inside Value Objects instead of using primitive types.
